---
title: 'CHECKING | The &quot;Free&quot; account, their hook into your wallet.'
date: '2016-05-08T06:08:00.0010000+00:00'
draft: true
blogger_blog: 'Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller'
blogger_status: 'LIVE'
blogger_post_id: '8380212211246904476'
blogger_filename: '/2016/05/checking-account-their-hook-into-your_7.html'
blogger_source: 'takeout-20260223T235336Z-3-001.zip:Takeout/Blogger/Blogs/Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller/feed.atom'
categories:
  - 'Banking'
---

<h4></h4><table align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" class="tr-caption-container" style="margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto; text-align: center;"><tbody><tr><td style="text-align: center;"><a href="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/howard-lake-save-our-cheques-campaign-c1e120c2100e.jpg" imageanchor="1" style="margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto;"><img border="0" height="223" src="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/howard-lake-save-our-cheques-campaign-140a03d933c4.jpg" width="400" /></a></td></tr><tr><td class="tr-caption" style="text-align: center;"><a href="https://www.flickr.com/photos/howardlake/4194853955/in/photolist-7oFHWc-8zhget-GeT99-muPh3-9PqQe5-opqo8N-6298sY-5YU2Et-tmFGu-r6FqM-iNzTZZ-mmw449-3K8jRm-6hn4Ac-nRMhAf-dr6ucu-y1j7x-598XcB-dhvYhZ-7zTXyt-dCcD7k-nodYFF-9dH1v-5Mvsks-4ZBDCc-bBiwNi-82p2v-4GQwNR-4R7CXN-o7XK9k-dEj5Yz-byKCAq-opdSYE-7YN12G-62NC7R-71BTAL-58JpPB-jxZ4s-fu3Loi-aZ2BzF-9e8Znv-6bonoB-nDnT2U-kJ4m1q-9pFb5Y-nqbyoV-c2wj5j-4QjzYd-cGwAP9-6GcQt9" target="_blank">Howard Lake "Save Our Cheques campaign"</a></td></tr></tbody></table><h4></h4><div><div><h4>Different Types of Checking Accounts</h4><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">I know I canâ€™t be the only person who thought thisâ€¦ but prior to my very first day working for a bank I had no idea there was more than one type of checking account.<br /><br />I figured a checking was a checking and a savings was a savings. I never thought of them as â€œproductsâ€ that could have different costs and benefits.<br /><br />Let's take a look at what a "Checking Account" really is.<br /><br /></div></div><h4>The primary product of a bank, is a checking account.</h4><div>A Checking Account is the product most people start with, and it's the hook to get you inside... so they can work on getting other products into your hands.<br /><br />This is a bit of a misnomer, as few people under the age of 40 still write checks (or Cheques as they are called in other countries). Most people now days use BillPay or DebitCard to take money out of the "Checking".<br /><br /><br /><div class="MsoNormal" style="-webkit-text-stroke-width: 0px; color: black; font-family: 'Times New Roman'; font-size: medium; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; font-weight: normal; letter-spacing: normal; line-height: normal; orphans: auto; text-align: start; text-indent: 0px; text-transform: none; white-space: normal; widows: 1; word-spacing: 0px;"></div><br /><div class="MsoNormal" style="-webkit-text-stroke-width: 0px; color: black; font-family: 'Times New Roman'; font-size: medium; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; font-weight: normal; letter-spacing: normal; line-height: normal; orphans: auto; text-align: start; text-indent: 0px; text-transform: none; white-space: normal; widows: 1; word-spacing: 0px;"><div style="margin: 0px;">In fact, for good reasons, check writing is becoming a thing of the past.&nbsp;</div><div style="margin: 0px;"><br /></div><div style="margin: 0px;">As it started a checking account was created as a place to store your money so that you could safely go about not having to carry loads of cash. Then you could write a â€œcheckâ€ which was a piece paper promising that cash to another person or company. &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp; &nbsp;&nbsp;</div></div></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><h4>~History~</h4><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">Fact Monster<a href="https://d.docs.live.net/6a09c1440da84011/Wolfe%20Family%20Conceptions%20Projects/!!Writing/Banking%20Basics/Website/Banking%20Basics%20for%20Believers%20stripped.doc#_ftn1" name="_ftnref1" title=""><!--[if !supportFootnotes]--><span style="color: black; font-family: &quot;times new roman&quot; , serif; font-size: 12.0pt;">[1]</span><!--[endif]--></a> Provides an&nbsp;interesting&nbsp;brief history here is an excerpt:<o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</div><blockquote class="tr_bq"><i>â€œAccording to most history texts, it probably wasnâ€™t until the early 1500s, in Holland, that the check first got widespread usage. Amsterdam in the sixteenth century was a major international shipping and trading center. People who had accumulated cash began depositing it with Dutch â€œcashiers,â€ for a fee, as a safer alternative to keeping the money at home. Eventually the cashiers agreed to pay their depositorsâ€™ debts out of the money in each account, based on the depositorâ€™s written order or â€œnoteâ€ to do so.â€</i></blockquote><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><h4>~Recent Times~</h4><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal">People used checks as the primary non-cash payment method up until the 1970â€²s when ATMâ€™s became widespread.<br /><br />The use of credit cards and debit cards didnâ€™t really take on as the preferred payment method until after the 1990â€²s really got underway.<br /><br />Many people from older generations are still without computers or cards, but banks around the world debate even accepting checks in the future. The burden of&nbsp;processing&nbsp;a check and recovering from potential fraud are higher than ANY other type of payment method.<br /><br /><blockquote class="tr_bq"><i>Just think how easy it is to order a new card VS getting a new account number and re setting up direct deposits etc.</i></blockquote></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><h4>~Interesting Facts~</h4><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">The following was copied from ehow.com<a href="https://d.docs.live.net/6a09c1440da84011/Wolfe%20Family%20Conceptions%20Projects/!!Writing/Banking%20Basics/Website/Banking%20Basics%20for%20Believers%20stripped.doc#_ftn2" name="_ftnref2" title=""><!--[if !supportFootnotes]--><span style="color: black; font-family: &quot;times new roman&quot; , serif; font-size: 12.0pt;">[2]</span><!--[endif]--></a>:<o:p></o:p></div><blockquote class="tr_bq">â€œ1971 -&nbsp;First true bank ATMs: Docutel introduces its Total Teller, the first true fully functioning bank ATM.&nbsp;</blockquote><blockquote class="tr_bq">1978 -&nbsp;The first IBM-compatible Diebold machine is installed at a bank in Indianapolis.</blockquote><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">The Expansion of Card Usage</div><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><blockquote class="tr_bq">Although the usage of the debit card at the POS has not surpassed the credit card as the preferred card medium of exchange, it has made tremendous strides in its commonality. A paper written by Robert M. Hunt of the Federal Reserve of Philadelphia notes that from 1980 to 2000, consumer purchases via debit card rose from zero percent to 12 percent, and purchases made via check fell from 86 percent to 50 percent. </blockquote><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">Future Implications<a href="https://draft.blogger.com/null" name="id.34e00aff9b0c"></a><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><blockquote class="tr_bq">If the recent move by British banks is any indication, the use of debit cards will skyrocket over in the next 10 years. Banks in Great Britain have agreed to stop accepting checks by 2018 (we shall see). They note factors such as the high relative cost of processing checks, coupled with the decline in merchants that accept checks as payment.</blockquote><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><h4>~Now Days~</h4><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal">Itâ€™s interesting that they noted that checks will not be accepted in Great Britain by 2018. Although there is no such plan in the United States (yet), it does underscore the growing trend away from checks and toward debit cards as the preferred method of direct payment. <o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">These days the check is not the&nbsp;preferred&nbsp;payment method of most institutions.<br /><br />Due to a new law called Check-21<a href="https://d.docs.live.net/6a09c1440da84011/Wolfe%20Family%20Conceptions%20Projects/!!Writing/Banking%20Basics/Website/Banking%20Basics%20for%20Believers%20stripped.doc#_ftn3" name="_ftnref3" title=""><!--[if !supportFootnotes]--><span style="color: black; font-family: &quot;times new roman&quot; , serif; font-size: 12.0pt;">[3]</span><!--[endif]--></a> many companies are now clearing checks electronically&nbsp;anyway&nbsp;to avoid the&nbsp;hassles.<br /><br />Have you ever tried to write a check at a store and the cashier ran it through then handed it back to you? Thanks to check-21 getting your â€œCanceled Checksâ€ or even â€œCheck Imagesâ€ is becoming more and more&nbsp;unlikely. Those who need good records for tax reporting are now best off using the bank-provided online bill payment services (BillPay) for the safest and best record keeping.<o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><h4>~My Account~</h4><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal">There are many checking account types offered by banks all going by different names. But here are the basics and categories:<o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal">All checking accounts at their most base level are for unlimited use of your money. You put money in and then use it as you need it, whether that is to make a purchase or pay a bill.<br /><br />The difference between accounts comes from what restrictions or&nbsp;privileges&nbsp;you get.<br /><br /><br /><ul><li>Some accounts offer free check orders, free bill payment services, even a small interest rate.&nbsp;</li><li>Other benefits may include a discount on services such as transfers to other banks or countries, waived stop payment fees, waived Non-Bank ATM fees, and more.&nbsp;</li><li>Sometimes the bank may give you an interest rate discount on a loan or a bonus on a CD.&nbsp;</li><li>Some banks honor their higher value clients with discounts on brokerage trades.&nbsp;</li><li>You could find you get a better insurance rate by having a long history and relationship with a specific bank.&nbsp;</li><li>You may find a loan easier to get by having a history with the bank you are applying through.</li></ul></div><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br />There can be packages and accounts available for Students, Military Members, and Employees of Member Companies, The Elderly or retirees may have accounts with&nbsp;privilege. Those with lots of money almost always have special accounts available. <o:p></o:p><br /><br />Once they have you firmly established into this "relationship" they can sell you all of these other "Products and Services". If you don't buy anything else, they won't be sad to see you leave. Buying other things is the only reason they still offer the Checking Account for a low cost, it actually costs the bank a lot of money to offer it.<br /><br />How does it cost them money?<br /><br />They have to process your transactions, protect you from fraud, have online services, call centers, tellers, etc, etc, etc. If you don't also borrow money from the bank, you are a cost to them (not a benefit).&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><h4>~Value~</h4><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</div><div class="MsoNormal"><blockquote class="tr_bq"><i>Why do people with more money get more?&nbsp;</i></blockquote></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">The fact is, despite all the talk about fees and overdrafts in recent times, banks make over 80% of&nbsp;their&nbsp;profit from less than 20% of&nbsp;their&nbsp;clients. The wealthier the client the more money they bring in profit for the bank.<br /><blockquote class="tr_bq"><i><br /></i><i>Consider: Wealthier Clients take out larger loans, and larger business loans.</i></blockquote></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><div class="MsoNormal">Rightly, the bank provides special things for people who bring this important business. Hopefully that helps take out some of the wonder about checking accounts.</div><div class="MsoNormal"><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal"><br /></div><blockquote class="tr_bq"><i><b>Side Note: This is also why when anyone with less than 1 million in the bank threatens to "take all my business elsewhere", the bank employee nods, smiles, and goes back to what they were doing without so much as a yawn when the person leaves, or hangs up.</b></i></blockquote><br /><br /><div><!--[if !supportFootnotes]--><b>The Insider</b><br /><br /><hr align="left" size="1" width="33%" /><!--[endif]--> <br /><div id="ftn1"><div class="MsoNormal" style="margin-left: 14.15pt; text-indent: -14.15pt;"><a href="https://d.docs.live.net/6a09c1440da84011/Wolfe%20Family%20Conceptions%20Projects/!!Writing/Banking%20Basics/Website/Banking%20Basics%20for%20Believers%20stripped.doc#_ftnref1" name="_ftn1" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><!--[if !supportFootnotes]--><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="color: black; font-family: &quot;times new roman&quot; , serif; font-size: 12.0pt;">[1]</span></span><!--[endif]--></span></a><span style="font-size: 8.0pt;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; http://www.factmonster.com/ipka/A0001522.html</span><o:p></o:p></div></div><div id="ftn2"><div class="MsoNormal" style="margin-left: 14.15pt; text-indent: -14.15pt;"><a href="https://d.docs.live.net/6a09c1440da84011/Wolfe%20Family%20Conceptions%20Projects/!!Writing/Banking%20Basics/Website/Banking%20Basics%20for%20Believers%20stripped.doc#_ftnref2" name="_ftn2" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><!--[if !supportFootnotes]--><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="color: black; font-family: &quot;times new roman&quot; , serif; font-size: 12.0pt;">[2]</span></span><!--[endif]--></span></a><span style="font-size: 10.0pt;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; http://www.ehow.com/about_6192272_history-atm-card.html</span><o:p></o:p></div></div><div id="ftn3"><div class="MsoNormal" style="margin-left: 14.15pt; text-indent: -14.15pt;"><a href="https://d.docs.live.net/6a09c1440da84011/Wolfe%20Family%20Conceptions%20Projects/!!Writing/Banking%20Basics/Website/Banking%20Basics%20for%20Believers%20stripped.doc#_ftnref3" name="_ftn3" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><!--[if !supportFootnotes]--><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="color: black; font-family: &quot;times new roman&quot; , serif; font-size: 12.0pt;">[3]</span></span><!--[endif]--></span></a><span style="font-size: 10.0pt;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Check_21_Act</span><o:p></o:p></div><div class="MsoNormal" style="margin-left: 14.15pt; text-indent: -14.15pt;"><span style="font-size: 10.0pt;"><br /></span></div><div class="MsoNormal" style="margin-left: 14.15pt; text-indent: -14.15pt;"><div class="MsoNormal" style="-webkit-text-stroke-width: 0px; color: black; font-family: 'Times New Roman'; font-size: medium; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; font-weight: normal; letter-spacing: normal; line-height: normal; orphans: auto; text-align: start; text-indent: 0px; text-transform: none; white-space: normal; widows: 1; word-spacing: 0px;"><div style="margin: 0px;"><br /></div><h4>The Insider is a BIG fan of Dave Ramsey:</h4><ul><li>Go read/listen/watch Dave Ramsey and he'll teach you how to do it right!</li></ul><ul><li>Listen to The Dave Ramsey Show (<a href="http://www.daveramsey.com/show/radio/" target="_blank">HERE</a>)</li></ul><ul><li>Or by his most popular book:&nbsp;<a href="http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/1595555277/ref=as_li_tl?ie=UTF8&amp;camp=1789&amp;creative=390957&amp;creativeASIN=1595555277&amp;linkCode=as2&amp;tag=your0f4d-20&amp;linkId=EH57Y7XH3LRAVQMF" rel="" target="_blank">(HERE) The Total Money Makeover: Classic Edition: A Proven Plan for Financial Fitness</a><img alt="" border="0" src="http://ir-na.amazon-adsystem.com/e/ir?t=your0f4d-20&amp;l=as2&amp;o=1&amp;a=1595555277" height="1" style="border: none; cursor: move; margin: 0px;" width="1" /></li></ul></div></div></div></div>
